figures.caption: Table 6: Statistics for the 30 datasets used in this paper. APSPD is all-pairs shortest path (geodesic) distance.

Table 7: Wall-clock times (mean ± std over 5 seeds, A10 GPU) on CityNetworks. GOBLIN’s train time (shown for LA only) is a one-time cost on Cora shared across all cities; inference includes the full BO operator search. GAT inference is a single forward pass; training is repeated per city per seed. The summary table gives total wall-clock across all 5 seeds and 4 cities, with and without GAT hyperparameter search (GOBLIN has no per-task search).
Figure 4: Range of best-performing operator on each task, out of all operators selected by GOBLIN across all 5 seeds.

Table 2: Mean ± std % accuracy (5 seeds) across 25 benchmark graph datasets. End-to-end models are trained directly on each task, so are generally easier than the zero-shot setting. Non-GOBLIN columns are reproduced from Finkelshtein et al. (2025). Heterophilic graphs are marked with an asterisk. First-/second-best results per dataset are color-coded.

Figure 5: The relationship between task range (as captured by GOBLIN) and GOBLIN performance improvement (∆ average % accuracy) over non-GOBLIN models (MPNNs, GraphAny and TSGNN, as in Tables 2 & 3) for 29 real-world benchmarks.
Figure 3: Range of GOBLIN on synthetic and real-world benchmark tasks (±σ over 5 seeds).

Table 8: Overall ranges (and for reference, validation accuracies), both fixed-W and black box, for 17 connected benchmark datasets. Computation of ρB uses 500 sampled nodes, or all of them, whichever is fewer. In most cases, ρB tracks mean geodesic distance fairly closely; ρW̄ provides a more accurate version of range—due to the fixed ρ ≤ 2 operators used, effective range of GraphAny on all tasks is < 1. All ranges are computed on the checkpointed instantiation of GraphAny from Zhao et al. (2024), trained on Wisconsin.
Table 4: Hyperparameter search space for GOBLIN, used in Figure 1 and Tables 2 & 3.
